narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—slightly above the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 39, which beats the dataset average of 36.70, you brought some impressive credentials to your law school application. Your family comes from an upper-middle income bracket, and you're planning to attend full-time, which is the norm for about 93% of students in the dataset. However, the admissions model, trained on past students' data to predict first-time bar exam success, estimated a 65% probability that you would fail the bar on your first try. This high risk of failure is why your application was rejected—the school uses this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the bottom tenth of your peers and strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure, as lower rankings like yours often signal challenges in sustaining high performance. Next in influence was your LSAT score, which worked in your favor by pulling the prediction toward bar passage, reflecting how your above-average result suggests good potential. Your Year 1 decile of 3 also nudged things toward failure, though less forcefully than Year 3, indicating an early pattern of grades below the typical mid-range decile of about 5-6. Compared to typical students who pass the bar, your academic deciles are noticeably lower, while your test scores align with or exceed passers, highlighting a gap between potential and consistent school performance. This combination outweighed your strengths, tipping the scales against you. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, as schools value demonstrated academic endurance for bar success. You might also seek targeted LSAT or bar prep tutoring to solidify those advantages, then reapply with updated transcripts showing improvement. Many students in similar positions have turned things around by addressing early weaknesses head-on, and with your strong starting scores, you're well-positioned to do the same.
